annotations.label: Dirac spinors

anchor.snippet: are vectors of four complex numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of a Dirac spinor; the underlying ℂ⁴ is just a generic vector space.

annotations.label: Zero-mass limit reduces to Weyl equation

anchor.snippet: in the limit of zero mass, the Dirac equation reduces to the Weyl equation

annotations.note: Neither the Dirac nor Weyl equation exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Klein–Gordon starting equation

anchor.snippet: His argument begins with the Klein–Gordon equation

annotations.note: The Klein–Gordon equation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linearization ansatz

anchor.snippet: the square root could be rewritten in a linear form

annotations.note: This physics ansatz has no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Anticommutation conditions on the variables

anchor.snippet: Dirac found that the variables must satisfy

annotations.note: The anticommutation relations are the Clifford-algebra defining identity, captured generally by `ι_mul_ι_add_swap`, but the specific α/β matrices are absent.

annotations.label: The Dirac equation

anchor.snippet: He thus arrived at the Dirac equation

annotations.note: The Dirac equation as a PDE is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Matrices form a Clifford algebra

anchor.snippet: they form a mathematical structure known of since the early 1880s, the Clifford algebra

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Clifford algebras generally via a quadratic form, but does not construct the specific matrix algebra of the Dirac matrices.

annotations.label: Dirac field

anchor.snippet: the key property of the field is that it transforms as a Dirac spinor

annotations.note: The Dirac field is a physics object with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Lorentz covariant Dirac equation

anchor.snippet: the Lorentz covariant formulation of the Dirac equation is given by

annotations.note: The covariant Dirac equation is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gamma matrices and the Dirac algebra

anchor.snippet: These are a set of four matrices generating the Dirac algebra

annotations.note: The Dirac algebra is a Clifford algebra Cl(1,3), which Mathlib has generically, but the gamma matrices themselves are not formalized.

annotations.label: Dirac algebra is a Clifford algebra

anchor.snippet: The Dirac algebra is a special case of the more general mathematical structure known as a Clifford algebra

annotations.note: Mathlib's general `CliffordAlgebra` is exactly the structure of which the Dirac algebra is a special case, though that instance is not built.

annotations.label: Dirac representation

anchor.snippet: is known as the Dirac representation

annotations.note: The specific Dirac basis of gamma matrices is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chiral representation

anchor.snippet: The first is the chiral representation, which is useful when decomposing

annotations.note: The chiral (Weyl) basis of gamma matrices is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Majorana representation

anchor.snippet: The second is the Majorana representation, for which all gamma matrices are imaginary

annotations.note: The Majorana basis of gamma matrices is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Adjoint Dirac equation

anchor.snippet: the adjoint Dirac equation can be found

annotations.note: The adjoint Dirac equation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lorentz invariance of bilinears

anchor.snippet: is not Lorentz invariant, but

annotations.note: Lorentz invariance of spinor bilinears is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-covariant (Hamiltonian) form

anchor.snippet: The Dirac equation can be rewritten in a non-covariant form similar to that of the Schrödinger equation

annotations.note: The Hamiltonian form of the Dirac equation is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dirac action

anchor.snippet: the Dirac action is given by

annotations.note: The Dirac action functional is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dirac equation as Euler–Lagrange equation

anchor.snippet: The equation then arises as the Euler–Lagrange equation of this action

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Euler–Lagrange field-theory machinery yielding the Dirac equation.

annotations.label: Dirac spinor as wave function

anchor.snippet: corresponds to a four-component spinor wave function describing the state of a Dirac fermion

annotations.note: The spinor wave function interpretation is physics with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Position probability density

anchor.snippet: Its position probability density , the probability of finding the fermion in a region of space

annotations.note: This quantum-mechanical probability density is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Operator-valued spinor field

anchor.snippet: the Dirac spinor is quantized to be an operator-valued spinor field

annotations.note: Quantized operator-valued fields are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Operator Dirac equation

anchor.snippet: The Dirac equation then becomes an operator equation describing the state-independent evolution

annotations.note: The operator form of the Dirac equation is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Grassmann-valued path integral field

anchor.snippet: is an anti-commuting Grassmann-valued field that only acts as an integration variable

annotations.note: The anticommuting Grassmann algebra is the exterior algebra, which Mathlib has, but Grassmann-valued fields and path integrals are not formalized.

annotations.label: Classical spinor field theory and SL(2,C)

anchor.snippet: The Dirac equation also arises in describing the time evolution of a spinor field in classical field theory

annotations.note: Although SL(2,ℂ) exists as `Matrix.SpecialLinearGroup`, classical spinor field dynamics is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Group representation

anchor.snippet: A representation is a particular choice of matrices that faithfully

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Representation k G V` (a monoid hom `G →* (V →ₗ[k] V)`) is the standard, more general formalization of a linear group representation.

annotations.label: Lorentz Lie algebra bracket

anchor.snippet: with the bracket for the Lorentz algebra given by

annotations.note: Mathlib has the general Lie bracket via `LieRing`, but the specific so(1,3) Lorentz algebra and its structure constants are not formalized.

annotations.label: Vector representation generators

anchor.snippet: the representation acting on real vectors is given by the six matrices

annotations.note: The explicit six so(1,3) generators in the vector representation are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lorentz transformation by exponentiation

anchor.snippet: The Lorentz transformation matrix can then be acquired from these generators through an exponentiation

annotations.note: Mathlib has the (matrix) exponential `exp`, but not the Lorentz-group-specific exp map from so(1,3) to SO(1,3).

annotations.label: Spinor representation generators

anchor.snippet: Another representation for the Lorentz algebra is the spinor representation where the generators are given by

annotations.note: The σ^{μν} spinor-representation generators built from gamma-matrix commutators are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Spinor representation is two-to-one (double cover)

anchor.snippet: is not one-to-one since there are two consistent choices

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `spinGroup` and its conjugation action on vectors, but the two-to-one covering of the (special) orthogonal group is not proven.

annotations.label: Lorentz covariance of the Dirac equation

anchor.snippet: The Dirac equation is a Lorentz covariant equation, meaning that it takes the form in all inertial reference frames

annotations.note: Lorentz covariance of the Dirac equation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lorentz invariance of the Dirac action

anchor.snippet: the Dirac action is Lorentz invariant, meaning that it is the same in all reference frames

annotations.note: Lorentz invariance of the Dirac action is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Global U(1) vector symmetry

anchor.snippet: acting on the phase of the spinor

annotations.note: This global U(1) phase symmetry of the Dirac field is a physics statement absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conserved vector current (Noether)

anchor.snippet: This has a corresponding conserved current that can be derived from the action using Noether's theorem

annotations.note: Noether's theorem and conserved currents are not formalized in Mathlib (matches for 'Noether' are all 'Noetherian').

annotations.label: Axial symmetry in the massless limit

anchor.snippet: known as the axial symmetry

annotations.note: The axial (chiral) symmetry is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Axial current

anchor.snippet: The current corresponding to the axial symmetry is given by

annotations.note: The axial current is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Axial current is a pseudovector

anchor.snippet: This transforms as a pseudovector , meaning that its spatial part is odd under parity transformations

annotations.note: Parity transformation properties of the axial current are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chiral anomaly obstructs gauging

anchor.snippet: at the quantum level it has a chiral anomaly that provides an obstruction towards gauging

annotations.note: The chiral anomaly is a quantum-field-theory result with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Stress-energy tensor from translations

anchor.snippet: Invariance under the four spacetime translations yields the Dirac stress-energy tensor

annotations.note: The Dirac stress-energy tensor is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lorentz currents

anchor.snippet: Invariance under Lorentz transformations meanwhile yields a set of currents indexed by

annotations.note: The Noether currents associated with Lorentz invariance are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Each component solves Klein–Gordon

anchor.snippet: gives rise to the Klein–Gordon equation for each component of the spinor

annotations.note: Neither the Klein–Gordon nor Dirac equation is in Mathlib, so this relation is absent.

annotations.label: Dirac solutions are Klein–Gordon solutions

anchor.snippet: any solution to the Dirac equation is also automatically a solution to the Klein–Gordon equation

annotations.note: This implication between the two equations is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Positive frequency plane wave solutions

anchor.snippet: The Dirac equation admits positive frequency plane wave solutions

annotations.note: Plane-wave solutions of the Dirac equation are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Negative frequency solutions

anchor.snippet: It also admits negative frequency solutions taking the same form

annotations.note: Negative-frequency Dirac solutions are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Momentum-space Dirac equation

anchor.snippet: Both these spinor polarizations satisfy the momentum space Dirac equation

annotations.note: The momentum-space Dirac equation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: General solution in chiral representation

anchor.snippet: In the chiral representation the general solution is given by

annotations.note: The general Dirac solution in the chiral basis is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalization to arbitrary dimension

anchor.snippet: it can be directly generalized to arbitrary dimension and metric signatures

annotations.note: Although Clifford algebras exist in arbitrary dimension in Mathlib, the Dirac equation itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Spinor covariant derivative

anchor.snippet: through the introduction of a spinor covariant derivative

annotations.note: The spinor covariant derivative is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dirac equation in curved spacetime

anchor.snippet: The Dirac equation in curved spacetime then takes the form

annotations.note: The curved-spacetime Dirac equation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nonlinear Dirac equation

anchor.snippet: gives rise to the nonlinear Dirac equation

annotations.note: The nonlinear Dirac equation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Breit equation

anchor.snippet: the Breit equation describes multi-electron systems interacting electromagnetically

annotations.note: The Breit equation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Two-body Dirac equation

anchor.snippet: The two-body Dirac equation is a similar multi-body equation

annotations.note: The two-body Dirac equation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dirac–Hestenes equation

anchor.snippet: A geometric reformulation of the Dirac equation is known as the Dirac–Hestenes equation

annotations.note: The Dirac–Hestenes equation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dirac–Kähler equation

anchor.snippet: Another related geometric equation is the Dirac–Kähler equation

annotations.note: The Dirac–Kähler equation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Decomposition into Weyl spinors

anchor.snippet: The Dirac spinor can be decomposed into a pair of Weyl spinors of opposite chirality

annotations.note: Weyl spinors and chirality decomposition are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reduction to coupled Weyl equations

anchor.snippet: the Dirac equation reduces to the pair of equations for the Weyl spinors

annotations.note: The reduction to coupled Weyl equations is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Massless decoupling into Weyl equations

anchor.snippet: in the massless limit the Weyl spinors decouple and the Dirac equation is equivalent to a pair of Weyl equations

annotations.note: Massless decoupling into Weyl equations is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Majorana equation

anchor.snippet: A closely related equation is the Majorana equation

annotations.note: The Majorana equation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Majorana spinor reality condition

anchor.snippet: These are spinors that satisfy a reality condition

annotations.note: The Majorana reality condition on spinors is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-relativistic limit is the Pauli equation

anchor.snippet: the Dirac equation reduces to the Pauli equation

annotations.note: Neither the Dirac nor Pauli equation is in Mathlib, so this limit is absent.

annotations.label: Pauli equation for a charged fermion

anchor.snippet: The equation describes a fermion of charge

annotations.note: The Pauli equation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gyromagnetic ratio equals two

anchor.snippet: the gyromagnetic ratio of the fermion described by the Dirac equation is exactly

annotations.note: The g=2 gyromagnetic ratio prediction is a physics result with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Elevating a global to a local symmetry

anchor.snippet: the Lagrangian can always be modified in a way to elevate a global symmetry to a local symmetry

annotations.note: The gauging procedure for symmetries is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauge field transformation

anchor.snippet: known as a gauge field, must be introduced

annotations.note: Gauge fields and their transformation laws are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauge covariant derivative

anchor.snippet: by replacing the derivative term with a new gauge covariant derivative

annotations.note: The gauge covariant derivative is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauged action gives quantum electrodynamics

anchor.snippet: introducing the kinetic term for the gauge field results in the action for quantum electrodynamics

annotations.note: The QED action is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Largest global symmetry U(N)

anchor.snippet: The largest global symmetry of this action is then given by the unitary group

annotations.note: Although U(N) exists as `Matrix.unitaryGroup`, the claim that it is the largest global symmetry of the Dirac action is physics not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauging requires an N-dimensional unitary representation

anchor.snippet: the symmetry being gauged must admit an

annotations.note: This gauging requirement is a physics statement not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauge field in the adjoint representation

anchor.snippet: which necessarily transforms in the adjoint representation of the gauge group

annotations.note: The adjoint Lie-algebra action exists in Mathlib (`LieAlgebra.ad`), but the non-abelian gauge field is not formalized.

annotations.label: SU(N) fundamental transformation of spinors

anchor.snippet: Spinors transforming in the fundamental representation transform as

annotations.note: SU(N) exists as `Matrix.specialUnitaryGroup`, but the fundamental transformation law of spinors is a physics statement absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: SU(N) gauge field transformation

anchor.snippet: which transforms in the adjoint representation as

annotations.note: The non-abelian gauge field transformation law is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quantum chromodynamics action

anchor.snippet: By also introducing the kinetic term for the gauge field, one constructs the action for quantum chromodynamics

annotations.note: The QCD action is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Yang–Mills field strength tensor

anchor.snippet: the Yang–Mills fields strength tensor is defined as

annotations.note: The Yang–Mills field strength tensor is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: SU(3) and SU(2) in the Standard Model

anchor.snippet: describes strong interactions of the quark sector of the Standard Model

annotations.note: SU(3) and SU(2) exist as `Matrix.specialUnitaryGroup`, but their Standard Model roles are physics not in Mathlib.
